publications.editedComment:
A dashboard with tabs in a row,
Lets community, shop, and mana each show.
Checkout return clues
Lose their query-string shoes,
While the forum says 'not ready to go.'

The Manager validates its stored tab, initializes Cart, User, and Nav state, and routes each accepted section to its own child surface. Mana and subscription success or cancellation messages are consumed once, then removed from the URL. Only successful subscription return refreshes user data. The cart summary is descriptive; no charge, item creation, or subscription activation occurs in this Component.

The storefront's real key is the Cart store. It initializes on mount, displays item count, formatted total, and any store error, and offers two hard-coded demo products: a twelve-dollar print and five-dollar token pack. Adding either creates a quantity-one CartItem with placeholder ArtImage id and image URL, then changes the Giftshop dashboard tab to `cart`. Review Cart performs only that tab change; Clear delegates to `clearCart`. The direct-giving link routes separately to `/giving` and explicitly says the site never touches that money. Product cards and checkout are still staging work. Nothing in this Component charges a customer or promises that the placeholder inventory exists.

Current quest state: storefront staging, demo cart items, and direct-giving route. Mount initializes the Cart store. The two showcase actions call `addItem` with fixed placeholder ids, URLs, prices, and quantity, then set the Giftshop dashboard tab to cart. Cart buttons display store totals, navigate to that tab, or clear existing items; the clear action is disabled when empty. Store errors are surfaced in the sidebar. The `/giving` link is independent and the copy states donations bypass this shop. No product fetch, inventory verification, payment initiation, or order placement occurs here. The next task is wiring real product cards—not pretending the demo parade is fulfillment.

The deed grants three controls while an animation is active: previous effect, next effect, and stop. The overlay covers the viewport but leaves pointer events disabled except on its centered control cluster. Its label comes from `activeEffect.label` with Animating as fallback, and the store's message appears beneath. Both effect navigation buttons delegate directly to the Animation store; stop does the same. Positioning accounts for the top safe-area inset. When the store is inactive, the entire Component vanishes. No effect is rendered here and no catalog is loaded. This land is the control easement above the animation, not the animation property itself.

This control strip only pops when `animationStore.isActive` is true. Prev and next buttons call the store's effect cyclers, the X calls stop, and the middle label reads the active effect or a generic Animating fallback. The message below is straight store state. Full-screen pointer events are off, then re-enabled on the actual controls so the overlay does not brick the page. Top placement respects the safe-area inset. That's the whole vibe: remote control, not renderer. No effect component, timer, persistence, or animation frame lives here. Clean little HUD, zero fake depth.

The spectacle appears only when the Animation store is active and the registry resolves the current effect id. That child is Teleported into a fixed, pointer-transparent layer and keyed by effect id so a change receives a fresh instance. A separate bottom status layer remains visible for every active state, even when resolution fails; it shows a spinner, effect icon, nested controls, and the store message or Generating art fallback. Both layers use the same short fade-and-scale transition. The registry decides what may mount, the child performs the effect, and the store owns activity. The hero's shell is intentionally hollow: presentation and coordination surround behavior that lives elsewhere.

One high flight path for effects, one lower perch for status. The registry maps `activeEffectId` to an approved Component; when both it and store activity exist, that child mounts full-screen in a Teleport layer with pointer input disabled. The status layer appears for any active animation and contains the spinner, catalog icon, interaction controls, and current message. Keying the child by effect id replaces the instance when the selection changes. Missing registry entries produce status without an effect rather than dynamic arbitrary loading. Transitions handle only entrance and exit. Small architecture, good altitude: store chooses, registry resolves, child renders, layer positions.

The intermission fills a viewport canvas with generated text and emoji life. Resize rebuilds the seaweed field. Timed spawners introduce fish, bubbles, and occasional larger creatures; each entity carries its own speed, phase, size, direction, alpha, or target data. Pointer movement influences nearby behavior. Clicking drops three to six pellets, creates a ripple, and gives many fish a temporary target near the click. Fish may be ordinary glyphs or the occasional robot. The animation loop updates, draws, and removes expired objects through requestAnimationFrame. Window resize, pointermove, and click listeners are installed on mount and removed with the stored frame on unmount. It is an interactive pause, not a static ASCII illustration.

The groove is a full canvas ensemble: right- and left-facing fish glyphs, bubbles, seaweed phrases, pellets, ripples, and roaming sea creatures. Every player gets randomized motion parameters, while frame counters schedule new arrivals. Mouse speed and position enter the improvisation; a click scatters food, expands a ripple, and invites many fish toward that spot. Seaweed is rebuilt for the viewport, bubbles rise and fade, fish bob and cross the stage, and creatures drift through less often. One requestAnimationFrame loop conducts the set. Resize and pointer listeners are paired with cleanup, and the frame is cancelled on departure. ASCII in name, canvas jazz in execution.
